reasoning: Arih1 (also known as TRIAD1) is an RBR‑type E3 ubiquitin ligase that modulates inflammatory signaling pathways, most notably the NF‑κB cascade. Several studies have shown that Arih1 can ubiquitinate key adaptor proteins such as TRAF2 or components of the IκB kinase complex, targeting them for proteasomal degradation and thereby acting as a negative regulator of NF‑κB activation. When Arih1 expression is reduced by CRISPRi, this inhibitory ubiquitination is diminished, leading to prolonged or enhanced NF‑κB signaling. CXCL3 (the protein product of the Cxcl3 gene) is a chemokine whose transcription is strongly induced by NF‑κB and AP‑1 downstream of pro‑inflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF‑α, IL‑1β). Consequently, loss of Arih1‑mediated repression is expected to increase NF‑κB activity and drive higher transcription of Cxcl3. Therefore, knockdown of Arih1 is most likely to cause an up‑regulation of Cxcl3 expression.
